title: Act I
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: SEREBRYAKOV, Alexander Vladimirovich, a retired professor. YELENA ANDREYEVNA, his wife, aged 27. SONYA, his daughter from his first marriage. VOYNITSKY, Ivan Petrovich (Uncle Vanya), brother of the professor's first wife. ASTROV, Mikhail Lvovich, a doctor. TELEGIN, Ilya Ilyich (Waffles), an impoverished landowner. MARINA, an old nanny. A WORKMAN.
ACT I

A garden. Part of the house and terrace can be seen. A table under an old poplar tree; garden seats and chairs. On one of the seats, TELEGIN is tuning his guitar. MARINA, an old woman, is sitting beside the samovar, winding wool. VOYNITSKY is pacing.
MARINA: (pouring tea) Have some, little father.
ASTROV: (reluctantly) I don't want any.
MARINA: A little drop of vodka, maybe?
ASTROV: No. I don't drink vodka every day. Besides, it's too hot. (Pause.) How long have we known each other, nurse?

MARINA: (thinking) How long? Lord help me remember. You came here—when was it?—Sonya's mother was still living. You came two winters before she died. That means eleven years. (After a pause.) Maybe more.
ASTROV: Have I changed much since then?
MARINA: Yes, a great deal. You were young and handsome then, and now you look old. You drink more, too.

ASTROV: Yes. In ten years I've become a different man. And what's to blame? Overwork, nurse. On my feet from morning to night, never a moment's rest, and then at night lying under the blanket afraid that someone will drag me off to a patient. In all the time I've known you I haven't had a single day free. How could I help getting old? And then life itself is boring, stupid, dirty. It grinds you down. (Pause.) Look, I'm surrounded by cranks, every one of them. Live with cranks for two or three years and you'll become one yourself. That's inevitable. (Twisting his long mustache.) Look at this mustache. What a silly mustache. I've become a crank. I haven't grown stupid yet, thank God, my brain still works; but my feelings have somehow grown blunted. I don't want anything. I don't love anything. Unless perhaps those forests. (He drinks tea.) How are you, nurse?
MARINA: Fine, thank you.
ASTROV: Nothing bothering you?
MARINA: Nothing.
(VOYNITSKY enters from the house. He has been sleeping and looks disheveled.)

VOYNITSKY: (yawning) Yes. (Pause.) Yes. Well, the professor and his wife have arrived and upset everything. I slept till twelve, drank coffee, then spent two hours trying to figure out where I was. Now it all comes back. It's him. He's arrived.
ASTROV: Have they arrived?

VOYNITSKY: Long ago. The professor and his wife. They upset the whole routine. I couldn't sleep. I'm irritable. I keep wanting to say something clever, something sharp, and then stopping. Oh, she is beautiful. Beautiful! I've never in my life seen anything so beautiful.
ASTROV: Tell me something.

VOYNITSKY: (sitting down) She's magnificent. (Pause.) I used to think: a wife like that—no, not like that—a woman like that—she was a dream, something remote. And now she's here, right here, sitting in the drawing-room. I can see her. She walks, she speaks, she smiles. She's the professor's wife. (Pause.) She's twenty-seven. He's sixty-two. How is it possible?
ASTROV: Have you been in love with her?

VOYNITSKY: She's my sister's widower's wife. It's complicated. But yes. I am. Isn't that absurd? Forty-seven years old and in love for the first time. (Pause.) I wasted my whole life. I managed this estate, I sent money to the professor, I lived like a mole. And for what? For him. I sacrificed my youth. I sacrificed everything. And what does he think of me? He thinks I'm a steward. A useful fool. He doesn't think of me at all.
ASTROV: Vanya, stop. You'll make yourself sick with all this.
VOYNITSKY: I know. I know. (Pause.) She's beautiful.

title: Act II
paragraphs: ACT II
The dining-room. Night. The WATCHMAN can be heard tapping outside. SEREBRYAKOV is sitting in an armchair by the window, dozing. YELENA is sitting beside him, also dozing.
SEREBRYAKOV: (waking) Who's there? Is it you, Sonya?
YELENA: It's me.
SEREBRYAKOV: You, Lenochka. This pain is unbearable.
YELENA: Your blanket has fallen. (She wraps it around his legs.) Let me shut the window.

SEREBRYAKOV: No, I'm stifling. I was dozing a little and I dreamed that my left leg didn't belong to me. When I woke up I had a dreadful, agonizing pain. This isn't gout, this is something worse. Something terrible, I'm sure of it.
YELENA: (quietly) It's all right. It will pass.

SEREBRYAKOV: You say that so calmly. But you're young and healthy and I'm an old man and—yes, I know, I know. I'm being unreasonable. I know you're all tired of me, tired of my illness, tired of these provincial nights. Only I—I too am tired. (He pauses.) I feel as if I've been living here for ten years. I haven't once set foot in the library since I arrived. I can't read, I can't think.
YELENA: It's the same for all of us. We're all just waiting—waiting for something. We don't know what.

SEREBRYAKOV: The fact is—you've all been spoiled by the professor. Accustomed to lectures, debates, conversations about art. And now I need to be carried to the privy at two in the morning. Is that amusing? No, it isn't.
(VOYNITSKY enters, carrying a candle. He stops in the doorway and listens.)
YELENA: (to SEREBRYAKOV) Your work—your ideas—you've given people something, and you have the right to expect—
SEREBRYAKOV: (fiercely) Yes! I gave everything! And what did I get in return? This house, this endless province, these provincial people with their provincial conversations. I'm dying here!
VOYNITSKY: (stepping in) Then let's all go to Petersburg together. The estate can run itself.
SEREBRYAKOV: Voynitsky, I'm in pain. I'm not up to jests.
VOYNITSKY: It's not a jest. I'm serious. Why must we stay in the country? We could all go.
SEREBRYAKOV: Please don't argue with me. Not now.

VOYNITSKY: (sitting down) Eleven years ago I managed this estate for the old professor—your father-in-law. (To YELENA.) I worked like an ox. I lived like a mole, and everything went to you. I gave you my youth. Now I'm forty-seven. I have nothing. I have wasted my life.
YELENA: (quietly) Ivan Petrovich, you're upsetting him.
VOYNITSKY: (not listening) I'm upsetting him? He's upset everything—upset my whole life!
SEREBRYAKOV: Go away. I cannot bear this tonight.
(VOYNITSKY goes. YELENA watches him go.)

YELENA: (to herself) What a house. What lives. And yet—and yet there is something beautiful here too, isn't there? The way the doctor talks about forests, about the future of the land—it means something. Something real, somewhere under all this. But I'm so tired.

title: Act III
paragraphs: ACT III
The drawing-room. Midday. VOYNITSKY and SONYA are alone. VOYNITSKY is looking out the window. SONYA is writing at the table.
VOYNITSKY: Mikhail Lvovich asked me to come and find you. He's saddling his horse. Go and say good-bye to him.
SONYA: (rising) Very well. (She hesitates.) Why is he leaving? He was just—
VOYNITSKY: He said he has patients. He's always working. That's what he says.
SONYA: He comes so seldom now. (She goes out.)
(YELENA enters.)
YELENA: You're here. Ivan Petrovich, I wanted to talk to you.
VOYNITSKY: (turning) What is it?

YELENA: (sitting) Listen. Please—you must be reasonable. You're a clever man. You must understand that to go on the way you've been going on—your complaints, your sighing, your resentment—it makes everyone miserable.
VOYNITSKY: You want me to stop? (He sits.)
YELENA: I want you to be—at peace. I think you know what I'm saying.
VOYNITSKY: (after a long pause) I've wasted my life. I'm not young, I'm not foolish enough to chase daydreams. But the past—when I think about what I might have been—
YELENA: Ivan Petrovich—
VOYNITSKY: I know. (He rises and goes to her.) Yelena Andreyevna. My only joy—you are my joy. I look at you and I—(He takes her hand.)
YELENA: (pulling away) Let go, please. This is—
VOYNITSKY: This is what? The truth? You are young and beautiful and your life is wasting away here. You know it. You must know it.
YELENA: (firmly) I am a married woman. Please.
(SEREBRYAKOV enters with SONYA.)

SEREBRYAKOV: Now, let us all sit down. I have something to say. (They sit.) The property, as you know, belongs to Sonya. It has been our family's estate for many years. But I have been thinking carefully, and I've come to a decision. Gentlemen, I propose to sell the estate.
(A pause.)
SONYA: Sell?
VOYNITSKY: Sell. I see. And then what?
SEREBRYAKOV: With the money from the sale I intend to purchase bonds, and to live on the income.

VOYNITSKY: You are proposing—you are seriously proposing—to sell this estate. My mother's estate. My sister's estate. The estate I have managed for twenty-five years. And go and live on the income in a city somewhere. (A pause.) This is remarkable. This is extraordinary. So this is it.
SEREBRYAKOV: I don't understand why you're upset.
VOYNITSKY: I've given you everything! (He rises.) Everything! And now you want to—you want to sell it out from under—(He stops. He picks up a paperweight.) You old professor. You worthless—
YELENA: (stepping between them) Ivan Petrovich—
(VOYNITSKY rushes out. A moment later there are two shots from the corridor. VOYNITSKY appears in the doorway with a pistol.)
VOYNITSKY: Missed. (Pause.) Missed again. (He drops the pistol and goes out.)
(A stunned silence.)
SEREBRYAKOV: (shakily) What—what was that? That madman—
YELENA: Take me away from here. Take me away. I cannot—I cannot stay—

title: Act IV
paragraphs: ACT IV
VOYNITSKY's room. It serves both as his bedroom and as the estate office. Evening. ASTROV is at the table packing his medical bag. VOYNITSKY is sitting beside the table.
ASTROV: (putting things away) Well, time for me to be off. My horses are ready.
VOYNITSKY: (not looking up) Yes.
ASTROV: You've had quite an adventure. You tried to shoot a man. You could be in prison.
VOYNITSKY: (with effort) Don't. Please.
ASTROV: (sitting down) No, really. You missed, didn't you? Point-blank and you missed. That's—well. There's something in that.
VOYNITSKY: I don't know why I did it. I was out of my mind. I wish I could explain it.
ASTROV: (gently) Vanya. We all have our moments. You had yours. It's over. Now you have to go on.
VOYNITSKY: Go on. (He looks up.) Doing what?
ASTROV: What you've always done. Manage the estate. Go on managing it.
VOYNITSKY: (bitterly) That's all there is for me.

ASTROV: That's not nothing. The estate is real. The work is real. It keeps things alive. (He pauses.) I believe in that, you know. Forests and fields and working land. I've planted thousands of trees in my life. Not one of them will survive to maturity in my lifetime, perhaps. But they'll be there. Someone else will see them.
(SONYA enters.)
SONYA: Mikhail Lvovich, the horses are ready. Papa says he wants to go immediately.

ASTROV: (rising) Yes, I'm coming. (To VOYNITSKY.) Life hasn't been what you wanted it to be. That's true. But you'll go on. You'll work. You'll drink your tea in the evenings and add up the accounts. And it will be very quiet and boring. And it will be life.
VOYNITSKY: Say something better than that.
ASTROV: (smiling sadly) If I could, I would. (He shakes his hand.) Good-bye.
(He goes out. SONYA stays. She comes and sits beside VOYNITSKY.)
SONYA: Uncle Vanya. Let's do something. Let's work. We haven't worked for so long. Let's work. (She takes his hand.)
VOYNITSKY: (looking at her) My poor girl.

SONYA: It will be all right. We'll go on. We'll write letters and add up accounts. We'll plant and reap and one day—Uncle Vanya, one day we'll rest. We'll rest and we'll know that we've endured, that we've had pity on ourselves and on those around us. And then God will have pity on us, and you and I—you and I—we'll know what rest means. We'll hear the angels singing. We'll see the sky like a jewel, and all our suffering will be dissolved in mercy.
(She wipes her eyes. The sound of the WATCHMAN tapping. TELEGIN plays softly on his guitar. MARINA is knitting.)
SONYA: We'll rest. (She presses her cheek against VOYNITSKY's hand.) We'll rest.
(VOYNITSKY sits quietly. The tapping of the WATCHMAN, the strumming of the guitar.)
CURTAIN.
